title: Tenancy Eviction (Punjab)
statute_text: Punjab Rented Premises Act 2009, Section 15 - Grounds for eviction: A landlord may seek eviction of the tenant if the tenant has failed to pay or tender rent within 30 days after the stipulated period; the tenant has breached a term or condition of the tenancy agreement; the tenant has violated an obligation under Section 13; the tenant has used the premises for a purpose other than agreed, or sub-let without written consent; the tenancy period has expired; or the landlord genuinely requires the premises for personal use.
elements_required: Valid tenancy relationship exists (written agreement strongly preferred)
At least one statutory ground under Section 15 is met
Landlord issued written notice stating grounds and a compliance/vacate period
Landlord files an eviction application with the Rent Tribunal if tenant does not comply
common_defenses: Eviction grounds are invalid or unproven - landlord must present clear evidence
Alleged breach has been rectified (e.g., overdue rent has since been paid)
Landlord failed to strictly follow notice requirements
Dispute over whether a valid tenancy relationship exists at all
procedural_notes: Rent Tribunals (established per district) have exclusive jurisdiction - not ordinary civil courts
Tenant may seek leave to contest; if granted, treated as written reply
Eviction cannot occur without a Rent Tribunal order
Appeal to District Court generally available (commonly cited ~30 days - verify)
Unregistered tenancy agreements can still support an eviction petition but may carry a penalty
example_scenario: A tenant stops paying rent for two months, citing a dispute over property repairs. The landlord issues written notice, the tenant fails to cure within the notice period, and the landlord files an eviction petition. Non-payment remains a valid statutory ground even if the tenant has a separate claim against the landlord for repairs.
review_status: NOT reviewed by a licensed lawyer - Punjab only, other provinces have separate statutes - verify current notice/appeal periods
